value.title:
What I bring
to the table
value.sub:
Digital experiences that engage users
and help products grow for real.
about.title:
Hi,
I'm Bruno
about.text1: I'm the one who stops the meeting when research gets skipped. I work on complex products — from architecture to visual — with a systematic process and AI workflows that cut time without cutting the quality of thinking.
about.text2: I've learned that the hardest design problems are never really design problems. They're about clarity, context, and who talks to whom. My job is to understand all of that before opening Figma.
contact.title:
Shall we build something
extraordinary?
contact.sub: Available for collaborations, freelance and full-time opportunities.

works-page.sub: From concept to launch — projects that helped products and platforms grow.
zu.hero.eyebrow: Smart Home · UX/UI Design · Interactive Prototyping
zu.hero.title:
The only smart home app
with a native digital twin
zu.hero.desc: A home automation app built around the physical structure of the house — granular control over windows, lights and climate without the complexity of Google Home or Apple Home
zu.brief.h:
Jared bought a Zuru house
and needs to set up his automations
zu.brief.p1: Zuru Tech builds cutting-edge smart homes — motorised windows with precise angle control, programmable RGB lights, intelligent climate. Jared has just purchased one of these homes and needs to configure his daily routines via app.
zu.brief.p2: Task 1: Automatically open the kitchen windows at 8:00 AM, with a specific opening angle (30°) and limited duration (15 minutes), to air the room before going to work.
zu.brief.p3: Task 2: Automatically set the living room lights to warm white at 6:00 PM to create a warmer, more welcoming atmosphere.
zu.brief.p4: The problem: existing smart home apps (Google Home, Apple Home) don't offer this level of granular control — or they bury it behind complex menus and technical configurations.
zu.sfida.h:
How to make smart control
as instant as a light switch?
zu.sfida.p: The challenge wasn't adding features — it was eliminating complexity. A smart home app must be faster than getting up and pressing a physical button. Otherwise it fails.
zu.sfida.li1: No mandatory floor plans — too much cognitive load for initial setup
zu.sfida.li2: Direct device access — maximum 2 taps for any action
zu.sfida.li3: Immediate visual feedback — animations showing the real state (window opening, light changing colour)
zu.sfida.li4: Smart presets — predefined moods instead of complex RGB sliders
zu.comp.h:
5 competitors analysed
by philosophy and UX patterns
zu.comp.p1: I analysed the main smart home apps to understand strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities for Zuru:
zu.comp.li1: Tesla App — Closed ecosystem (car + home + energy). Simple UI for complex systems, but zero granular device control and no home digital twin.
zu.comp.li2: Loxone — Integrated building automation with proprietary HW+SW. Excellent granular control (angle, position, schedule) and "if X then Y" logic without code, but requires a certified partner for installation.
zu.comp.li3: Josh.ai — Luxury AI-first home. Natural Language to describe automations in plain words, context-aware per room, but requires costly professional installation.
zu.comp.li4: Google Home — Multi-brand ecosystem with universal compatibility. Device list per room (familiar pattern) and simple routines, but no digital twin and limited granular control.
zu.comp.li5: Apple Home — HomeKit with privacy focus. Device list by room/category, all on-device, but closed ecosystem (HomeKit only) and no home digital twin.
zu.comp.p2: Zuru's competitive advantage: It's the only one that starts with a native digital twin of the house. No competitor has this information built in. The UI should build on top of this — making technically complex things visible and simple.
zu.ux.p1: To define the optimal entry point, I considered three factors:
zu.ux.li1: 1. Established patterns — Apple Home and Google Home use device lists organised by room. It's a familiar, immediate pattern: the user instantly sees what they can control without navigating a floor plan.
zu.ux.li2: 2. Jared's behaviour — Jared is thinking "I want to set up the kitchen window". A clear list with "Kitchen Windows" as the first entry lets him reach the target device directly with no intermediate steps.
zu.ux.li3: 3. Implementation simplicity — A device list is simpler to implement and maintain than an interactive floor plan, allowing focus on the quality of automations and granular control.
zu.ux.quote: "I chose the device list as the entry point to balance pattern familiarity, access speed, and implementation simplicity. It lets Jared complete the required tasks in the fewest taps possible."
zu.ic.h:
Animations that show
the real device state
zu.ic.p: I designed high-fidelity interactive components for the main controls:
zu.ic.li1: Window Slider — Isometric view of the window opening progressively (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°) as the user drags the slider. Immediate visual feedback of the real state.
zu.ic.li2: Light Mood Cards — Instead of complex RGB sliders, 4 predefined presets (Warm Evening, Focus, Cinema, Relax) with colour preview and description. Tap to change mood, separate brightness slider.
zu.ic.li3: Duration Pills — Quick selection of automation duration (5m, 10m, 15m, 30m, 1h) with opening/closing time preview.
zu.coreflow.p: Optimised for single, fast tasks like Jared's:
zu.coreflow.li1: 1. Home Dashboard — Device list organised by room. Tap "Kitchen Windows" for direct access.
zu.coreflow.li2: 2. Device Control — Opening angle slider (0–90°), real-time window visualisation, "New Automation" button.
zu.coreflow.li3: 3. Automation Setup — Simplified form with 3 fields: Time (8:00 AM), Duration (15 min), Angle (30°). Selectable week days.
zu.coreflow.li4: 4. Confirmation — Summary of the created automation with the option to edit or return to home.
zu.plusflow.p1: For expert users who want to orchestrate complex scenarios:
zu.plusflow.li1: Scene Builder — Canvas to add multiple devices and define simultaneous actions. E.g.: "Good Morning" = Kitchen windows 30° + Living room warm lights + Coffee maker ON.
zu.plusflow.li2: Advanced Scheduling — Weekly calendar with hourly timeline. Drag & drop scenes to specific days/times.
zu.plusflow.li3: Conditional Logic — "If-Then" builder for conditional rules. E.g.: "If temperature > 25° Then open windows 60°".
zu.plusflow.p2: Rationale for Core/Plus split: Two flows for different complexity levels. Core is optimised for single, fast tasks, while Plus offers power for users who want complex multi-device scenarios.
zu.ds.h:
Complete token system
with Manrope and 4px spacing
zu.ds.p1: I built a complete design system with CSS variables to ensure consistency and scalability:
zu.ds.li1: Typography — Manrope font (Regular → ExtraBold). Scale from 10px (Caption/S) to 30px (Heading/XXL) for optimal readability on digital screens.
zu.ds.li2: Colors — Semantic palette with Background (#EDECEA), Card (#FFFFFF), Brand Red (#E5173F), Status colors (Green, Orange, Blue).
zu.ds.li3: Spacing — System based on multiples of 4px (4px, 8px, 12px, 16px, 20px, 24px, 32px) for pixel-perfect alignment.
zu.ds.li4: Border Radius — Semantic values: r-card (8px), r-pill (100px), r-input (6px) for consistency across similar components.
zu.ds.li5: Shadows — 3 levels (sm, default, lg) for clear visual hierarchy.
zu.ds.p2: Every component is responsive and adapted for iPhone (393×852) and iPad (834×1194) with device-specific padding and font sizes.
zu.proto.p1: I built high-fidelity interactive prototypes in Figma using Component Sets and Smart Animate:
zu.proto.li1: Window Slider — 4 states for iPhone + 4 for iPad (8 variants total)
zu.proto.p2: Total: 26 component states with Smart Animate transitions for immediate visual feedback.
zu.results.li1: Direct device access — room list instead of mandatory floor plan
zu.results.li2: Immediate visual feedback — animations showing the real state
zu.results.li3: Simplified setup — no mandatory floor plans, just a device list by room
zu.results.li4: Smart presets — predefined moods instead of complex configurations
zu.next.li1: User Testing — Tests with 5–8 users to validate the Core flow. Task: "Set up automatic kitchen window opening at 8:00 AM". Metrics: completion time, errors, satisfaction.
zu.next.li2: Interactive Slider Implementation — Complete the window component variants (0°, 30°, 60°, 90°) and connect the slider with Interactive Components for real-time visual feedback.
zu.next.li3: Plus Flow Expansion — Design Scene Builder and Advanced Scheduling for power users. Validate with users already using complex automation systems (e.g. Home Assistant, Loxone).
zu.next.li4: Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA audit: colour contrast, keyboard navigation, screen reader support. Special attention to sliders and granular controls.
zu.next.li5: Dev Handoff — Document the design system in Figma with variables, components, and auto-layout. Prepare technical specs for the API (automation data format, device control endpoints).
zu.learn.li1: Complexity is not a feature — it's a bug
zu.learn.li2: The interactive floor plan is visually impressive, but for most users it's unnecessary cognitive overhead
zu.learn.li3: Animations are not decoration — they are essential feedback
zu.learn.li4: Zuru's competitive advantage (native digital twin) must be invisible to the end user
td.hero.title:
Redesigning the value
of planting a tree
td.hero.desc: An immersive physical experience to restore emotional weight to a digital gesture — and turn passive users into active reforestation advocates
td.ctx.h:
Treedom targets 10 million trees —
but conversion is declining
td.ctx.p1: Treedom is one of the world's leading reforestation platforms: it lets users plant and adopt real trees in deforestation-risk areas, with the goal of reaching 10 million trees by 2025.
td.ctx.p2: The problem: gifting a tree has lost emotional value over the years. A digital action that feels abstract, geographically distant, and hard to perceive as real. The platform is suffering directly — a disruptive solution was needed, not an incremental redesign.
td.sfida.h:
How do you make a digital gesture
meaningful again?
td.sfida.p: The challenge wasn't improving the interface — it was recovering the tangibility of an action that digital had made too abstract. A tree planted 10,000 km away can't be seen, felt, or remembered.
td.sfida.li1: The act of planting a tree has lost its emotional weight
td.sfida.li2: Geographic distance cancels the perception of real impact
td.sfida.li3: Post-purchase engagement is low — no reason to return
td.sfida.li4: A conceptual breakthrough was needed, not a visual improvement
td.proc.p: I collaborated in a team applying the Double Diamond methodology: divergence in the research phase, convergence in insight synthesis, then ideation and prototyping. Each phase informed the next — the concept is built on real data, not intuitions.
td.res.p1: We conducted an extensive benchmark — competitors, sustainability success cases and disruptive references from other sectors — to understand what truly generates emotional engagement.
td.res.p2: The 9 semi-structured interviews with target users revealed behavioural and motivational patterns that no quantitative data would have uncovered.
td.res.li1: Competitive and inspirational benchmark on sustainability platforms
td.ins.v1: Fun is the key — People commit to environmental action when it's enjoyable, not when it's a moral responsibility. Fun lowers the barrier to entry.
td.ins.v2: Natural escapism — Users actively seek moments of disconnection from hyper-connected life. Nature is a mental refuge, not just an ethical value.
td.ins.v3: Digital contribution perceived as unreal — Planting a tree online feels less concrete than any physical action. Distance cancels the sense of impact.
td.persona.h:
Lorenzo — the hyper-connected creative
looking for a break
td.persona.p: Content designer, natural early adopter. He loves technology but suffers from the dark sides of a too-digital life: stress, dependency, creative blocks. His antidote is moments of isolation and contact with nature — which he seeks more and more actively.
td.persona.v1: Find inspiration · Disconnect from the digital routine · Discover new, tangible experiences
td.persona.v2: Environmental gestures feel empty · Hyper-connectivity causes stress · Can't find experiences that genuinely blend tech and nature
td.persona.v3: Treedhome is exactly what Lorenzo is looking for — a physical, tech-driven, immersive experience with real impact on the planet
td.vision.h:
Treedhome — a dome
that brings the forest to the city
td.vision.p1: Treedhome is a travelling physical structure: an immersive dome that replicates the sensory experience of a real forest — spatial sounds, integrated fragrances, LED walls with live video from the forests where users have planted their trees.
td.vision.p2: It's not just an installation. It's the physical touchpoint that transforms an abstract digital gesture into a memorable experience — and converts curious visitors into active reforestation supporters.
td.vision.li1: Travelling — it moves between Italian squares and fairs, bringing Treedom's mission to where the people are
td.vision.li2: Interactive — the user personalises the experience from their own device in the "Make Your Forest Play" section
td.vision.li3: Sustainable — built with CO₂-absorbing materials. The structure is coated with Gosh paint: an innovative mineral-based coating capable of capturing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, turning the dome itself into an active emission-reduction tool
td.proto1.p1: Inside the dome the user is enveloped by spatial sounds, natural fragrances and live video from the real forests where Treedom plants trees every day. Not a simulation: an authentic connection to nature.
td.proto1.p2: The first experience is free and guided by the app. At the end, a natural CTA invites users to unlock the sounds at home too — the first step toward conversion.
td.proto2.h:
Make Your Forest Play —
the forest at home
td.proto2.p1: "Make Your Forest Play" extends the Treedhome experience beyond the dome. Subscribed users listen to the real sounds of the forests where they have planted their own trees — building a personalised sound environment in their daily life.
td.proto2.p2: The interface is composable: each user creates their own natural symphony by choosing forest, season and intensity. A digital product with concrete emotional impact — and a reason to return every day.
td.proto2.v1: Chosen forest · Trees planted by the user · Season and intensity preferences
td.proto2.v2: Personalised soundscape — real sounds from the user's own forest
td.proto2.v3: Reduces stress · Keeps the bond with the forest alive · Strengthens post-purchase retention
td.nexts.h:
Scaling the concept
and new use contexts
td.nexts.li1: 3D Visor — the immersive experience could evolve with XR for home access to the forest without fixed hardware
td.nexts.li2: Cultural adaptation — integrate local sounds, fragrances and visual elements for each geographic context
td.nexts.li3: B2B applications — corporate settings for employee wellbeing and healthcare for recovery and stress relief
td.learn.h:
What I learned
about sustainability and design
td.learn.li1: Disruptive design is conceptual before it's visual — the real break is in the experience model, not the look
td.learn.li2: A digital action loses value when it lacks physicality — phygital bridges that gap powerfully
td.learn.li3: Fun is the key — environmental commitment grows when it stops being a moral burden and becomes pleasure
td.learn.li4: The physical touchpoint is the strongest converter — it converts and retains better than any push notification
ral.hero.title:
Renting between strangers,
as natural as sending a message
ral.hero.desc: A P2P rental app designed to turn distrust into trust — 5 weeks of Design Thinking, qualitative research, and AI features to radically lower the barrier to entry.
ral.sfida.h:
Turning an unusual gesture
into something natural
ral.sfida.p1: Renting between private individuals is a powerful idea, but often held back by doubts and distrust. The challenge was to design an experience that conveyed trust, simplicity and safety, turning an unusual gesture into something natural.
ral.sfida.p2: RentAll needed to make renting as easy as sending a message — even for those who have never shared anything with a stranger. Technology had to become a bridge, not a barrier.
ral.met.p: The project was developed following an approach inspired by Design Thinking, combining qualitative and quantitative tools to obtain solid, design-useful insights.
ral.met.ph1d: Understanding real needs, obstacles and behaviours through interviews, surveys and competitive context analysis.
ral.met.ph2d: Translating insights into design opportunities, defining key features and information architecture.
ral.met.ph3d: Building high-realism prototypes and testing with real users to validate solutions and identify further improvement areas.
ral.ric.h:
Two guiding questions
to understand real behaviour
ral.ric.p: The research focused on two main aspects, often overlooked by existing platforms:
ral.ric.li1: The conditions that drive someone to rent an item rather than buy it — when renting becomes the rational choice and what the emotional trigger is.
ral.ric.li2: The fears and resistance that prevent people from making their own items available — the 'owner' side is the most fragile from a trust perspective.
ral.ric.li3: Semi-structured interviews — Individual sessions to explore deep motivations, fears and behaviours. Focus on real episodes: "Tell me the last time you needed something for temporary use."
ral.ric.li4: Quantitative survey — Questionnaire to measure propensity to rent, trust threshold towards strangers and the importance of guarantees and checks.
ral.ric.li5: Competitor analysis — In-depth study of Fat Llama, Subito and Wallapop to identify critical gaps in trust, safety and service accessibility.
ral.ins.d1: People accumulate items they no longer use — often after a hobby or lifestyle change. The barrier isn't the availability of items, but trust in the sharing process.
ral.ins.d2: The owner side is the most vulnerable: the fear that the item will be damaged or not returned systematically blocks adoption. No competitor had solved this convincingly.
ral.ins.d3: Creating a listing — photographing, describing, setting a price — is seen as a tiring and risky activity, not a natural gesture. Initial friction is the main drop-off point.
ral.disc.h:
Unused items, missing trust,
overly complex setup
ral.disc.p1: When changing hobbies or lifestyle, people tend to accumulate unused items. Lenders are often worried about the condition in which the item will be returned and about the reliability of the other party.
ral.disc.p2: In parallel, analysis of competitors like Fat Llama, Subito and Wallapop revealed systemic weaknesses in trust, safety and service accessibility. No one had solved the owner side convincingly.
ral.disc.q: The problem isn't just finding someone to rent — it's convincing the owner it's worth doing. Without trust, the marketplace doesn't take off.
ral.viaig.h:
Three acts, two protagonists,
one trust platform
ral.viaig.p: I built a narrative customer journey in three acts covering both sides of the market — the owner listing for rent and the renter searching — plus the critical mid-rental check moment.
ral.ai.h:
Two features to build trust
from both sides
ral.ai.p: The two most critical insights — "listing is tiring" and "I'm afraid they'll damage my item" — directly generated two AI features that break down the marketplace's main barriers.
ral.learn.d1: In peer-to-peer contexts, technology alone isn't enough. Every feature must address a real user fear — and this is doubly true when it comes to personal belongings.
ral.learn.d2: Working with high-realism prototypes helped me get concrete feedback even on advanced features like Smart Scan and Mid-Rental Check — users react much more authentically.
ral.learn.d3: I strengthened my ability to simplify complex interfaces, transforming them into familiar and intuitive experiences. Perceived simplicity is the real differentiator in a trust marketplace.
ral.nexts.h:
Where the next iteration
would take it
ral.nexts.li1: Guarantee and insurance system — Integration of a guarantee and insurance coverage system to increase platform trust for those lending valuable items — the main adoption barrier for the 'owner' segment.
ral.nexts.li2: Advanced reputation system — Verified profiles, rental history and bidirectional ratings to build a reputation system that further reduces initial distrust and incentivises virtuous behaviour.
lm.hero.title:
Designing a scalable
e-learning experience
lm.hero.desc: Design of a training platform to support complex content, personalisation and growth over time
lm.ctx.p: I worked on the design of Leone Master School, a digital training platform with mentorship paths led by real entrepreneurs and professionals. The project required managing a complex ecosystem — content, instructors, paths, free and paid users — while maintaining clarity and scalability over time.
lm.prob.h:
Not a UI problem —
a system problem
lm.prob.p: The logged-out home displayed all courses indiscriminately, without differentiating free from paid, without a guided path, without active onboarding. Returning users had no immediate resumption point.
lm.prob.li1: Free and paid courses not visually differentiated
lm.prob.li2: No guided onboarding — no video, no test, no assigned path
lm.prob.li3: No clear progression for the new user
lm.prob.li4: High initial drop-off and low activation
lm.prob.li5: Returning users with no immediate resumption point
lm.app.p: I approached the project as a system problem, not an interface one. Each phase of the framework has a precise strategic goal — conversion, activation, orientation, engagement, retention — and invisible logic operating in the background.
lm.app.li3: The returning user needs to resume without friction
lm.sol1.p: I designed the logged-out home as a progressive conversion funnel, where every element reduces uncertainty and lowers the barrier to entry.
lm.sol1.li1: Welcome video explaining the platform and reducing uncertainty
lm.sol1.li2: Course previews by category (management, real estate, personal growth, AI)
lm.sol1.li3: Entrepreneur instructors section to build credibility
lm.sol1.li4: "Sign up for free" CTA that lowers the barrier to entry
lm.sol1.li5: Registration form at the bottom of the page
lm.sol2.h:
Guided onboarding:
not a tutorial, a system
lm.sol2.p: I designed an onboarding that combines guided content and preference collection, turning first access into an active and relevant moment.
lm.sol2.li1: Hero video — reduces uncertainty and creates context before any choice
lm.sol2.li2: Preference test — collection of goals and interests (Duolingo-style)
lm.sol2.li3: Free course assignment — personalised path based on responses
lm.sol3.p1: I designed a home based on the user's state. The "Continue your mentorship" component eliminates re-orientation friction — the user sees exactly the course, section and lesson to resume from, with a single click.
lm.sol3.p2: The live session calendar maintains engagement over time, showing upcoming live lessons with date, time and a direct-access CTA.
lm.sol4.p: I structured the sell page for each path to communicate value before access, reducing uncertainty through information, credibility and guided navigation.
lm.sol4.li1: Hero with 4 key info boxes: Training, Duration, Format, Full Time
lm.sol4.li2: Introductory video with a dedicated instructor for that module
lm.sol4.li3: Interactive map with tooltips guiding the user through modules
lm.sol4.li4: Side accordion programme with numbered modules
lm.sol4.li5: Path instructors section with photo + role cards
lm.sol5.p1: Each module is paired with a real expert instructor — active entrepreneurs and managers who teach from daily practice. No theorists, no academic professors.
lm.sol5.p2: I designed instructor pages with 6 cards: Role & specialisation, Key skills, Quote, Education, Values, Teaching mission.
lm.sol6.p: I introduced personalisation logic to adapt the experience based on user behaviour and state.
lm.sol6.v1: Preference test · User behaviour · Progress state · User type (new / active / returning)
lm.sol6.v3: Assigned free courses · Adapted home · Continue component · Progressive upsell free → paid
lm.sol6.v4: Progressive — non-invasive · Relevant — based on real data · Scalable — adapts over time
lm.ds.p: I designed reusable components to guarantee consistency and scalability of the system.
lm.ds.li1: Course card (with free / paid badge and integrated progress bar)
lm.ds.li2: Continue component (thumbnail + section + lesson + resume CTA)
lm.ds.li3: Module list (states: completed / in progress / to start + dedicated instructor)
lm.learn.li2: Onboarding is not a tutorial — it is the first moment of personalisation
lm.learn.li3: The home must adapt to the user's state, not be the same for everyone
lm.learn.li4: The Continue component is more powerful than any algorithmic recommendation
lm.learn.li5: Differentiating free and paid is not just commercial — it is a UX principle
lm.pres.p: The project was presented on stage at ICMD — one of the biggest events in Italy's training sector — in front of 1,500 professionals. A moment that validated not only the design work, but the entire systemic approach to the platform.
tiaa.hero.eyebrow: Career Media · UX Design · Content Strategy
tiaa.hero.title:
Rethinking
Job Discovery
tiaa.hero.desc: UX design for a content-driven platform helping Gen Z choose where to work, not just where to apply
tiaa.ctx.h:
A platform to choose,
not just search
tiaa.ctx.p1: There Is An Alternative is a media platform with a precise mission: giving power back to candidates in the job-choice process. Not a job listings portal, but a system that centres company stories — who they are, how they work, what they believe.
tiaa.ctx.p2: I joined the project to design the UX and layout of the platform — translating the client's vision into a navigation system, content architecture and interfaces consistent with the editorial positioning.
tiaa.prob.h:
Not a listing problem —
a trust problem
tiaa.prob.p: Traditional portals treat work as a catalogue of open positions. Each listing is a card: title, requirements, salary, candidates. But this model ignores the most important question a candidate asks: "Will I be in the right place for me?"
tiaa.prob.li1: Poor navigation experience, identical for all companies
tiaa.prob.li2: Company culture completely invisible to the user
tiaa.prob.li3: No authentic content — only corporate copy and generic job descriptions
tiaa.prob.li4: Total disconnection between editorial content and job opportunities
tiaa.prob.li5: Low trust in the process: users don't recognise themselves in existing formats
tiaa.prob.ins: Gen Z is not simply looking for work. They seek contexts in which to recognise themselves. They don't just evaluate the position — they evaluate the company, the people, the values, the day-to-day working environment.
tiaa.app.p1: The client's vision was clear: not a restyling, but a paradigm shift. The point was not to make company profiles prettier — it was to completely rethink the discovery model.
tiaa.app.p2: My work was to translate that vision into UX architecture. The framework guiding the experience follows three principles in sequence: content attracts and engages, storytelling builds trust, job integration converts interest into action.
tiaa.app.li1: Content to attract and engage — video, photos, real stories before job listings
tiaa.app.li2: Storytelling to build trust — companies have a voice and identity, not just open positions
tiaa.app.li3: Job integration to convert — listings enter the natural flow of discovery
tiaa.sol1.p: Every company page is designed as a story, not a profile card. The user doesn't arrive on a list of requirements — they arrive on an identity.
tiaa.sol1.li1: Immersive hero — ambient photography that communicates the company atmosphere
tiaa.sol1.li2: TIA Insider video — real employees talking about daily work, not promotional spots
tiaa.sol1.li3: Editorial grid — interviews, testimonials, moments of company life
tiaa.sol1.li4: Key data sidebar — employees, founding year, average age, offices, values
tiaa.sol1.li5: Contextual CTAs — "Explore the company" and "See open positions" placed naturally
tiaa.sol2.h:
Finding the right company
without searching for it
tiaa.sol2.p1: One of the main problems with traditional portals is that they require users to already know what to look for. TIA introduces a discovery model — not just search. Navigation is designed to surprise, not only to filter.
tiaa.sol2.p2: The home presents a centralised search bar, but the visual content just below — featured companies, curated editorially — invites passive discovery. The user explores before searching.
tiaa.sol2.li1: Home with search + editorially curated "featured" company grid
tiaa.sol2.li2: Find a company: filters by sector, city, size with visual focus on content
tiaa.sol2.li3: Fluid navigation: from "looking for a job" to "exploring where I want to be"
tiaa.sol2.li4: Media section: editorial content separate but connected to company discovery
tiaa.sol2.li5: Reduced friction: no list of requirements as the first visible content
tiaa.sol2.v1: Gen Z behaviour · authenticity expectations · need to identify before applying
tiaa.sol2.v2: Company storytelling + visual content + content-job integration
tiaa.sol2.v3: Discovery as a narrative and immersive experience · conscious and motivated application
tiaa.sol3.p: Job listings don't interrupt the narrative flow — they are part of it. A TIA listing is never separate from the company context that hosts it.
tiaa.sol3.li1: Company identity always visible in the listing (logo, sector, culture)
tiaa.sol3.li2: Visual tags by type: full time, hybrid, occasional
tiaa.sol3.li3: Role description with editorial tone, not bureaucratic
tiaa.sol3.li4: Direct link to company page for those wanting more context
tiaa.sol3.li5: "Apply now" CTA at the end of a journey, not as the sole page objective
tiaa.res.p: The result is a more engaging, credible experience aligned with the expectations of the new generation of users. TIA positions itself as a real alternative to traditional portals, shifting the focus from "finding work" to "choosing where to work".
tiaa.learn.p: This project taught me that in complex platform design, the real problem is rarely technical or visual — it is relational.
tiaa.learn.li1: The problem wasn't access to information — it was how people build trust
tiaa.learn.li2: Designing for Gen Z means designing for authenticity, not efficiency
tiaa.learn.li3: Content is not an accessory — it is the product itself
tiaa.learn.li4: Discovery is more powerful than search: a user who explores converts better than one who filters
tiaa.learn.li5: Integrating editorial and functional requires a system vision, not two separate departments
tiaa.nexts.li1: A/B test different company page models to measure engagement
tiaa.nexts.li2: Feed personalisation based on sector and browsing behaviour
tiaa.nexts.li3: Mobile optimisation — video-first experience on smartphones
tiaa.nexts.li4: Company dashboard: analytics to measure the impact of content on applications
cls.hero.title:
From showcase site
to conversion machine
cls.hero.desc: Complete optimisation of a luxury short-term rental property website in Rome: information architecture, conversion rate optimisation and behavioural analysis with real data.
cls.ctx.h:
A luxury site without
a conversion strategy
cls.ctx.p1: Colosseum Luxury Suite is a luxury accommodation a few steps from the Colosseum, with two distinct apartments: Suite 26 and Suite 41. The site was built on a Framer template and functioned as a simple showcase, without a defined conversion strategy.
cls.ctx.p2: The main problem: potential guests arrived on the site but didn't have enough information to make a purchase decision. There were no dedicated pages for each suite, trust elements were missing, and the calls to action were generic and barely visible.
cls.ana.p: Before touching the design, I analysed real user behaviour through three tools in combination: Google Search Console, GA4 and Microsoft Clarity.
cls.ana.p2: Users were looking for the suites but couldn't find them. The Clarity heatmap showed that the element with the most clicks overall (8.89% of total) was the title "Colosseum Luxury Suites" — users were clicking looking for a dedicated page that didn't exist.
cls.ana.p3: Clarity also detected a rage click rate of 1.49% and dead clicks at 6.93% — signals of frustration with the interface. Average scroll depth stopped at 52% with an activity time of 51 seconds.
cls.prob.t1: No dedicated pages for the suites
cls.prob.d1: Users were looking for a destination, they found a section. No in-depth context, no complete gallery, no visible pricing. The most frequent click was a link that led nowhere.
cls.prob.d2: The Airbnb Superhost badge was present but not prominent. In luxury hospitality trust precedes interest — it cannot be a secondary detail. The guest must see the credentials before even reading.
cls.prob.d3: The booking buttons didn't communicate value and didn't appear at the right moments in the reading funnel. A guest who finished reading had no obvious action to take.
cls.prob.d4: Suite 26 and Suite 41 were presented as interchangeable variants. A guest had no tools to understand which was better suited to their needs — and visits were almost identical (30 vs 29).
cls.opt.d1: I designed and developed dedicated pages for /suite-26 and /suite-41 as custom React JSX components in Framer. Each page includes a gallery with navigation, full services list, pricing, real reviews and double CTA.
cls.opt.d2: Repositioning of the Airbnb Superhost badge in the hero section with stars and number of reviews immediately visible. Addition of a location section with map and distances from key points of interest.
cls.opt.d3: Direct booking via Airbnb for those ready to book, WhatsApp contact for those still evaluating. The two channels intercept different moments in the decision process without forcing a premature choice.
cls.opt.d4: Optimisation of meta title and description for both suites with specific keywords for the luxury short-term rental Rome niche. The dedicated suite pages also create additional indexable surface the previous site lacked.
cls.res.p: One week after the optimisations went live, the first signals are positive. GA4 data shows growth across all fronts, while the frustration signals detected by Clarity have disappeared completely.
cls.res.note: Data note: results are measured 7 days after launch. Google takes 4–8 weeks to re-evaluate optimised pages — organic results will be more readable in the next update.
cls.ref.p: This project confirmed how luxury hospitality has specific conversion dynamics: the user doesn't book impulsively, they build trust before even gathering information. Every design choice — from the position of the Superhost badge to the gallery structure — was aimed at supporting that process.
cls.ref.q: Sometimes the designer's job is not to invent new patterns, but to listen to what users are already trying to do. The 8.89% of clicks on the title were a clear request: "give me a dedicated page."
adc.hero.title:
Redesigning the entry
into the world of work
adc.hero.desc: How do you win the trust of a generation that doesn't trust impersonal processes? Research, UX strategy and content in service of connection.
adc.ctx.h:
Gen Z visits Adecco.
But doesn't register.
adc.ctx.p1: The challenge wasn't technical — it was relational. Young people aged 18 to 26 arrive on the site, look around, and leave. Not because they aren't looking for work, but because they don't feel they're in the right place: the language is too formal, the processes seem designed for someone else, and human contact is almost absent.
adc.ctx.p2: Adecco gZ was born to address this gap — rethinking the digital experience for a target often overlooked by traditional platforms, making the first approach to the world of work clearer, more engaging and more human.
adc.ric.p: I conducted an in-depth analysis of Gen Z digital behaviour and their friction points with the world of work — not as a demographic segment, but as people with concrete needs and precise expectations.
adc.ric.li1: Analysis of 4 competitors: Joinrs, Glassdoor, JobTech, Manpower — tone of voice, onboarding, visual content
adc.ric.li2: Definition of a realistic persona (Dani, 20 years old) built on real digital behaviours, not stereotypes
adc.ric.li3: Qualitative analysis of the existing Adecco blog: generalist content, skewed towards a 30–45 target
adc.ric.li4: Quantitative research on Gen Z keywords: CV, interviews, smart working, salary, work-life balance
adc.ins.h:
They're not just looking for work.
They're looking for a place to belong.
adc.ins.p: Gen Z is not hard to engage — they're hard to deceive. They want motivational but authentic tone, short visual content, and above all the perception of real human contact. The user needs to be guided step by step, not abandoned in a system designed for others.
adc.str.h:
A flow that accompanies,
not that abandons.
adc.str.p: I designed a step-by-step experience that transforms the application from a bureaucratic action to an immersive journey. Every moment of the flow is designed to reduce anxiety, build trust and bring the user closer to the company before the interview.
adc.sol.h:
Content as UX.
48 pieces. 3 months. 4 directions.
adc.sol.p: The solution wasn't just a new interface — it was an editorial system designed with a UX mindset. A plan of 48 pieces of content in 3 months, divided into 4 thematic areas: the world of work, company values, new technologies, training.
adc.sol.li1: Short articles, mobile-optimised, with call to actions integrated into the flow
adc.sol.li2: Motivational, serious tone of voice, without fake enthusiasm
adc.sol.li3: Every piece of content was a designed touchpoint — not filler
adc.sol.li4: 4 thematic areas: work, company values, technology, training
adc.cand.h:
A job offer that truly
speaks to Gen Z.
adc.cand.p: I redesigned the job offer page to make it less bureaucratic and more human. Fewer requirements in list form, more context about the working environment. The language is direct, the tone inclusive, the structure designed for someone reading on mobile in 30 seconds.
adc.az.h:
Not just an offer.
A place you'd want to be.
adc.az.p: I designed the company page as a narrative space — not a list of benefits, but a window into how work really happens. Content generated by the company itself, real reviews, team videos. Trust built before the interview.
adc.home.h:
A home that welcomes,
not that intimidates.
adc.home.p: The new Adecco gZ home is designed for first impact. No forms straight away, no HR jargon. Just a clear message about why it's worth staying — and a guided path towards the first step.
adc.learn.h:
Content doesn't support UX.
Content is UX.
adc.learn.li1: In certain contexts, writing and tone are just as important as layout
adc.learn.li2: A user's trust is also built through a well-chosen word
adc.learn.li3: Collaborating with marketers teaches you to translate editorial objectives into design decisions
adc.learn.li4: Defending UX choices requires arguments that also speak to those who don't do design
adc.nexts.li1: Matching system based on interests and soft skills, not just qualifications
adc.nexts.li2: Integration of live events and real-time content in company pages
adc.nexts.li3: Dedicated area for those without experience, with guided paths and progressive milestones
